destination.vibe: 90 miles north of Penn Station by Amtrak, Rhinebeck sits at the center of the best road cycling in the Northeast — 300-year-old country lanes through orchards and river estates, the Hudson at the bottom of every hill, the Catskills on the horizon, and the Shawangunk Ridge one bridge-crossing away.

flights.outbound_dates: July–August flexible (Tue/Wed cheapest; 4+ daily nonstops DTW→JFK)
flights.notes: DTW→JFK nonstop. Best bike policy of any major carrier: bikes treated as oversized checked bag at the standard rate (~$35/way), not the $150 penalty others charge. Confirm 115 linear inch / 50 lb limit before booking. Average $179–$217 RT in July — book 40 days out for best pricing. If bringing a soft bike bag, Delta is the clear best option; if shipping via BikeFlights ($80–120/way), United becomes cheapest total.

flights.outbound_dates: Early August (Tue Aug 4 cheapest; ~daily nonstops DTW→EWR)
flights.notes: DTW→EWR (Newark). Cheapest ticket but United's bike fee is ~$150 each way — wipes out the savings vs. Delta unless you're shipping your bike separately. Check united.com baggage policy before booking. EWR is easy for Penn Station: Newark AirTrain + NJ Transit (~40min, ~$18).

flights.outbound_dates: Late July (Jul 29 from $129 one-way; flexible Aug return from ~$149 OW)
flights.notes: DTW→JFK nonstop. $100 bike handling fee + standard bag fee on top of the ticket price, and an 80-linear-inch / 50 lb hard case limit that's tight for most bike bags. Best used as a backup if Delta is sold out.

transportation.name: AirTrain + Subway — JFK to Penn Station
transportation.description: JFK → AirTrain to Jamaica → A or E train to Midtown Penn Station · bikes allowed on NYC subway
transportation.notes: Bikes are allowed on the NYC subway — carry/fold vertically. Large bike bags fit but it's crowded in rush hour; aim off-peak. Alternative: LIRR from Jamaica to Penn Station (~$11.50, ~30min total instead of 55min) if you want to move faster.

transportation.name: Amtrak Empire Service — Penn Station NYC → Rhinecliff
transportation.description: Penn Station NYC to Rhinecliff station · the scenic route up the east bank of the Hudson River
transportation.notes: The Empire Service runs 3–4x daily to Rhinecliff. Rhinecliff is a 1873 Victorian depot right on the river bank, 1 mile below Rhinebeck village (uphill). Reserve bike space at booking — July fills fast. The last hour of the train ride follows the Hudson River with Catskills views across the water. Return: same line south; bike reservation required again.

transportation.name: Amtrak — Rhinecliff → Poughkeepsie (Shawangunk day)
transportation.description: 5 stops south to Poughkeepsie · used for the Shawangunk Ridge day ride
transportation.notes: Take this instead of riding 30+ miles south just to reach the Walkway Over the Hudson. Poughkeepsie station is 10 minutes from the Walkway entrance by bike.

lodging.name: Beekman Arms & Delamater Inn
lodging.highlights: America's oldest continuously operating inn (est. 1766) on Market Street — roll out the front door onto back roads, free breakfast, free parking, walking distance to every restaurant and farm stand in town.

lodging.name: The Wick
lodging.highlights: Converted 1860s candle factory on Hudson's gallery-lined Warren Street, 2 miles from the Empire State Trail — complimentary breakfast, call ahead for secure bike storage, Lil' Deb's Oasis and Cafe Mutton are both around the corner.

lodging.name: Mirbeau Inn & Spa
lodging.highlights: The only lodging in the valley explicitly built for the cycling-then-recovery pattern: complimentary loaner bikes, a full spa, pools, on-site dining — plus you're one mile from every back road in Dutchess County.

itinerary.overview: The Hudson Valley has been the best road cycling in the Northeast for decades, and cyclists have been the only ones who know it. The roads around Rhinebeck, Red Hook, and Tivoli are genuinely quiet — traffic stayed on Route 9 while the real roads looped through farm country behind it. You fly into JFK, take the Amtrak Empire Service 90 miles north to Rhinecliff (a Victorian depot right on the riverbank), roll off the train, and you're immediately in back-road territory. Across the Hudson, the Shawangunk Ridge offers some of the best sustained climbing in the Northeast: 15% pitches through white conglomerate escarpments, accessed via the Walkway Over the Hudson in Poughkeepsie. In between: orchards, river views, Bard College campus roads, and the galleries and restaurant scene of Hudson — the most interesting small city in New York State.

days.title: Arrive — DTW → JFK → Rhinecliff
days.morning: Fly DTW→JFK (Delta, 1h52m nonstop). AirTrain to Jamaica station (~8min, $8.50), then A or E subway to Penn Station (~35min, $2.90). Or LIRR from Jamaica to Penn Station (~$11.50, faster). Amtrak Empire Service to Rhinecliff — book the bike space at amtrak.com when you book the ticket ($10 fee, July fills fast). The train follows the Hudson River for the last 45 minutes; you can see the Catskills across the water.
days.afternoon: Rhinecliff station is a 1873 Victorian depot right on the river bank — one of the prettiest train stops in the Northeast. The mile from Rhinecliff up to Rhinebeck village is the one climb of the day. Check in, assemble bike if needed. Bread Alone Bakery (45 E Market St, closes 5pm) for a pastry and coffee.
days.evening: Dinner at Terrapin (farm-to-table since 1998, seasonal menu rotating around local producers — the anchor of the Rhinebeck food scene) or casual at Aba's Falafel for something quick and inexpensive.

days.title: Rhinebeck → Bard → Tivoli → Red Hook — 34mi / 55km, ~1,100ft / 335m
days.morning: Introductory loop — enough climbing to feel it, quiet enough to enjoy it. Head north from Rhinebeck on Route 9G, then duck onto the back roads through Bard College campus (42.0264, -73.9089 — technically private, cycling tolerated, beautiful estate roads). Continue north to Tivoli. Tivoli General (52 Broadway) opens early: get a bialy before they sell out. They make the best bialys in the Hudson Valley and they know it.
days.afternoon: Lunch at GioBatta Alimentari (48 Broadway, Tivoli) — market and deli counter, wildly good food, Ruth Reichl is a known regular. Not on any 'best of' list because it doesn't market itself. Head south from Tivoli toward Red Hook, stopping at Montgomery Place Orchards (Route 9G, Red Hook — open Wed–Sat 9–6, Sun 9–5 from mid-June): 60+ apple varieties, apple cider donuts, pies. Return to Rhinebeck via Red Hook village and back lanes.
days.evening: Gigi (Mediterranean with Hudson Valley ingredients, lively, no attitude) or Rhinebeck Farmers' Market if it's Sunday (10am–2pm, 23 E Market St — build your week around this if possible).

days.title: River Road Long Loop — 52mi / 84km, ~1,400ft / 427m
days.morning: The big day. Early start — 6:30 or 7am — to beat the summer heat. Head southwest from Rhinebeck toward the river, connecting to River Road south. River Road is the open secret of Hudson Valley cycling: flat, paved, river views, almost no cars. Stop at Migliorelli Farm Stand (668 River Rd, open daily 9–6) for mid-ride fuel — third-generation farm, over 130 varieties of fruit and vegetables. The river corridor in the morning light, before the heat builds, is as good as it gets.
days.afternoon: The route loops back north through inland farm roads and rolling terrain before returning to Rhinebeck. The accumulated climbing is modest per pitch but persistent — plan for it. Water and snacks at Rhinebeck village before the final stretch.
days.evening: You've earned a proper dinner. Terrapin in Rhinebeck, or if you want to take the Amtrak one stop south: Cafe Mutton (757 Columbia St, Hudson — crépinettes, beef tartare, excellent brunch too, Wed–Fri evenings, Sat–Sun brunch only).

days.title: Hudson Day — Warren Street, Olana, Lil' Deb's
days.morning: Rider's choice: either ride 25 miles south on Route 9G to Hudson (flat to rolling, decent shoulder, moderate traffic), or lock the bike and take Amtrak one stop south (~12 minutes, ~$12 each way — reserve bike space if riding). Circles Bagels in Hudson for breakfast — sourdough bagels with toppings like spicy whitefish, shiso, and chili crisp.
days.afternoon: Olana State Historic Site (5720 Route 9G, 1.5 miles south of Warren Street on a hill above the river) — Frederic Church's 250-acre Persian-inspired estate with a view of the Hudson and Catskills he spent his career trying to capture on canvas. The grounds are free to walk; the house tour is ticketed. Then Warren Street: 50+ antique dealers occupying intact 19th-century storefronts, Carrie Haddad Gallery (Hudson Valley's best photography gallery, est. 1991), and Ornamentum (contemporary jewelry worth seeing even if you're not buying).
days.evening: Lil' Deb's Oasis (747 Columbia St, Thursday–Sunday only, no reservations). Arrive 15 minutes before opening — there will be a line. Tropical comfort food using Hudson Valley ingredients: whole fried fish, fried plantains, octopus tostadas, shrimp aguachile. James Beard-nominated. Nothing else in the region like it.

days.title: Shawangunk Ridge — 46mi / 74km, 2,800ft / 853m (from New Paltz)
days.morning: Cross the river. Take Amtrak south to Poughkeepsie (~35min from Rhinecliff, ~$12), then ride 10 minutes to the Walkway Over the Hudson — a 1.28-mile pedestrian/cycling bridge 212 feet above the river, longest elevated pedestrian bridge in the world, free, open 7am–sunset. Cross to Highland (west bank) and ride 12 miles south to New Paltz. The Shawangunk Ridge is visible the whole way — a white conglomerate escarpment rising sharply out of the farmland.
days.afternoon: Two options: (A) Shawangunk Ridge Road Ride — 46 miles paved, 2,848ft elevation, two major climbs over the ridge with 15% pitches, starts and ends in New Paltz; (B) Mohonk/Minnewaska State Park carriage roads — 35+ miles of no-car gravel through 8,000 acres, $10 day-use fee, some of the finest gravel cycling in the Northeast. Both are legitimate; pick based on your bike. Descend to New Paltz, Amtrak back north from Poughkeepsie to Rhinecliff.
days.evening: Recovery dinner. Something simple in Rhinebeck.

days.title: Farmers' Market Morning → NYC → DTW
days.morning: If it's Sunday: Rhinebeck Farmers' Market (10am–2pm, 23 E Market St) — widely considered the best in the Hudson Valley. Pick up provisions for the train. If not Sunday: final easy spin on whatever back road you haven't done yet. Pack and disassemble the bike. Amtrak Empire Service south to Penn Station (reserve bike space). The view downriver on the return trip is different from the upriver view — still good.
days.afternoon: AirTrain and Delta back to DTW. Home by dinner.

tips: River Road goes by multiple names as it runs parallel to the Hudson — it's labeled differently on different maps and changes names at town lines. Download your route from Mid Hudson Bicycle Club (midhudsonbicycleclub.com) or Komoot before leaving; cell signal on back roads can be spotty.

July heat in the Hudson Valley is genuine — mid-80s to low 90s with humidity. Aim to finish riding by noon. Start at 6:30am, which means you're done with 50 miles before the worst of the day. The tree canopy on back roads helps but doesn't eliminate the heat.

Lil' Deb's Oasis (Hudson) is Thursday–Sunday only and takes no reservations. Go at opening or expect a significant wait. If you miss dinner, their lunch is slightly easier to snag. Plan your Hudson day for a Thu–Sun.

GioBatta Alimentari in Tivoli (48 Broadway) doesn't have much of an online presence and isn't on most 'best of' lists. Go for lunch — it's the best food in the valley for its size and price point. Tivoli General (bialys, early hours) is directly across the street.

The Walkway Over the Hudson in Poughkeepsie is the key to accessing the Shawangunks without a car. Free, open 7am–sunset, bikes allowed. From the Highland (west) end, it's 12 flat miles south to New Paltz on decent roads. Don't skip the Shawangunk day — it's a completely different character from the east-bank back roads.

Mid Hudson Bicycle Club (midhudsonbicycleclub.com) has 700+ published routes on Ride with GPS covering Dutchess and Ulster Counties at every skill level. This is the single most useful resource for route planning in the region.

Rhinebeck Farmers' Market (Sundays, 10am–2pm) is worth building your itinerary around — it's the social center of the valley on summer weekends and the produce is excellent. Buy here, eat at Bread Alone, and you'll be provisioned for a solid day on the bike.
Montgomery Place Orchards (Route 9G, Red Hook) is explicitly bike-accessible and has an extraordinary selection even in early summer. The apple cider donuts are not optional.
